weather.tip: Overcast with a 59% rain chance today and a warmer 31°C high — keep a light rain jacket or umbrella handy for a possible afternoon shower, and carry water since it'll get muggy between showers.

around.headline: Bengaluru class 8 student's death renews scrutiny of school harassment
around.summary: Reports today say a class 8 student died by suicide, with the family alleging harassment at school played a role. If you or a young person you know is struggling, the Kiran mental health helpline (1800-599-0019) is free and available 24/7.

around.headline: Bengaluru customer alleges food-order dispute turned violent
around.summary: A customer says an outlet staffer hit him on the head with an iron rod after a mix-up over his food order. Heated service disputes can escalate fast — step back and complain through official channels rather than confronting angry staff in person.

around.headline: Metro Green Line hit by another technical glitch
around.summary: BMRCL confirmed a technical glitch disrupted Green Line services today, the second such snag for commuters in recent days. Build in extra buffer time and check BMRCL's updates before you head out if you rely on this line.

around.headline: Abandoned-vehicle removal drive begins, footpath-parking rules still unclear
around.summary: GBA's crackdown on abandoned vehicles blocking roads and footpaths is now underway, though officials haven't clarified how it treats footpath-parked (but not abandoned) vehicles. If you park on a footpath or public roadside, don't assume it's safe from this drive.
around.source: The Times of India / The Hindu / Deccan Herald

around.headline: US opens H-1B/PERM visa fraud probe, names Cognizant
around.summary: A US labour watchdog has launched a fraud investigation into H-1B and PERM visa filings, with Cognizant specifically mentioned. Worth watching if you or your employer sponsors US work visas out of Bengaluru's IT sector.

around.headline: India may face pressure as US revives Russia sanctions push
around.summary: A renewed US sanctions push on Russia could put India in a tough spot given its reliance on discounted Russian oil imports. Keep an eye on fuel prices in the coming weeks if this escalates.

street_smart.title: A food order comes out wrong and the counter staff gets aggressive when you complain. Voices are rising. What do you do?
street_smart.based_on: Real case reported today: a Bengaluru customer alleges a food outlet staffer hit him on the head with an iron rod after a dispute over a wrong order.
street_smart.situation: Your order arrives wrong — missing items, or not what you paid for. You point it out at the counter, but the staffer is already stressed from a busy shift and gets defensive. The exchange starts getting loud and other customers are watching. What do you do?

choices.text: Raise your voice back and refuse to leave until they fix it right in front of everyone.
choices.feedback: This is exactly the dynamic that turned violent in today's case: matching an already-agitated staffer's tone and cornering them in front of an audience is the spark, not the solution. Public confrontations over a billing or order dispute can escalate faster than either side intends.

choices.text: Pay for the wrong order without comment, leave quietly, and post about it online later.
choices.feedback: This keeps you safe in the moment, but you get no refund or correction, and an online review alone doesn't create a record any authority can act on if you decide to pursue it further.

choices.text: Threaten to call the police right there to pressure them into fixing it immediately.
choices.feedback: Bluffing with authority can provoke someone who's already worked up, and if they call your bluff you've escalated the situation without actually having backup on the way.

choices.text: State the issue calmly once, ask for a manager, and if it's not resolved, step back, note the outlet name and order details, and file a complaint via the National Consumer Helpline (1915).
choices.feedback: Correct. Removing yourself from a heated in-person exchange prevents it from escalating, and a documented consumer complaint — backed by your receipt or order screenshot — carries real weight without putting you at physical risk.

takeaways: If a service dispute starts escalating, disengage physically before it turns confrontational — matching aggression is what turns billing arguments into assault cases.
Document everything (receipt, order screenshot, staff/outlet name) before you leave — that's what makes a National Consumer Helpline (1915) complaint stick.
If you're actually threatened or hit, call 100 immediately and treat it as a police matter first — physical safety outranks the billing dispute.

quiz.q: Which Bengaluru Metro line was disrupted by a technical glitch according to today's reports?
quiz.why: It's the second such technical snag for commuters in recent days, per today's coverage.

quiz.q: Today's news says a US labour watchdog's H-1B/PERM visa fraud probe named which IT company with a major Bengaluru presence?
quiz.why: PERM is the US Department of Labor's permanent labor certification process, a separate track from the H-1B visa itself.

quiz.q: What is the goal of the civic drive that began in Bengaluru according to today's reports?
options: Removing abandoned vehicles from roads and footpaths
quiz.why: Officials still haven't clarified whether footpath-parked (but not abandoned) vehicles will also be towed under the same drive.

quiz.q: In India, the National Consumer Helpline for filing complaints against faulty products or services can be reached at which number?
quiz.why: 1930 is the separate national cybercrime helpline, 108 is for medical emergencies, and 100 is for police — each helpline covers a different kind of emergency.

quiz.q: The long-running water-sharing dispute between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu centres on which river?
quiz.why: The Cauvery Water Management Authority was set up specifically to oversee sharing of the river's water between the two states.
